narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 14.50, Year 1 school decile of 2nd, Year 3 decile of 1st, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This close call tipped the scales toward rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 academic decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.5th decile and signaled weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, very close to the average of 3.21, which nudged the model toward expecting a bar exam pass. Your middle family income bracket also helped mildly, as it aligned with patterns where students from similar backgrounds sometimes outperform expectations on the bar. Less pivotal but still considered were your unusually low LSAT score of 14.50 compared to the average 36.70, and your full-time status, which is common among 93% of students and didn't sway things much either way. Overall, your Year 3 grades stood out as the biggest concern, overshadowing your solid undergrad work and background, especially when stacked against students who typically rank higher in later years and boast stronger LSATs. While your profile shows promise in some areas, it fell short of the stronger academic trajectories that predict bar success for most admitted applicants. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive academics. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor.
